caveats: a: The occupation mix is for NAICS 541800 and includes advertising and related services with different workflows.
a: O*NET task importance does not measure time, wage weight, feasibility, or existing automation.
a: The Muck Rack survey reports individual tool use rather than the share of agency labor technically substitutable.
a: The injected labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and may be distorted where agency receipts include third-party campaign or event pass-through.
rho: Tool use and self-reported speed or quality are not audited implementation or labor savings.
rho: Muck Rack mixes agency and in-house professionals and does not isolate LMM firms.
rho: IPRN covers member agencies across more than 22 countries and does not state a respondent count on the fetched page.
rho: Current high adoption reduces the remaining not-yet-implemented opportunity even as it supports future workflow acceptance.
e: No source measures eligibility under the frozen recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM lens.
e: The injected firm count uses a 15.65% broad Business & Consumer Services margin and may misclassify high-margin counsel boutiques or lower-margin execution-heavy agencies into the EBITDA band.
e: The code includes lobbying and political consulting as well as conventional PR, creating customer and cyclicality variation.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than PR agencies or the LMM band.
s5: PRovoke's transaction universe is not restricted to the United States, control deals, or the frozen EBITDA band.
s5: Many observed targets below $6 million in fees are likely below the LMM EBITDA threshold.
s5: Founder and rainmaker dependence can make nominal agencies difficult to transfer without long earn-outs.
q: The PRSA guide establishes fee structures but does not measure their prevalence or commercial retention.
q: The guide predates current AI adoption and modern procurement practices.
q: Retention varies sharply between hourly media-relations execution, fixed projects, crisis availability retainers, lobbying, and strategic counsel.
d5: The BLS output forecast is for NAICS 541800, not public relations agencies alone.
d5: Occupation growth includes in-house PR staff and workers outside agencies.
d5: IPRN is a global member network rather than a representative US LMM sample.
d5: The estimate holds quality and the current service basket constant, excluding wholly new AI-visibility services.
o: No source directly measures the future operator-required share of PR service demand.
o: Agency survey statements about human judgment and strategic trust may reflect provider optimism.
o: Rapid improvement in autonomous monitoring, content generation, journalist targeting, sentiment analysis, and crisis simulation could push the value below the range.
o: Lobbying and political consulting are more operator-dependent than routine publicity, widening intra-code variation.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Businesses, institutions, nonprofits, public bodies, political interests, and individuals buy ongoing counsel and campaign execution through monthly or annual retainers, hourly fees against budgets, fixed project fees, and hybrids. Repeatability is strongest in corporate reputation, media relations, public affairs, crisis preparedness, executive positioning, and integrated communications.
lens.excluded_activities: Advertising agencies, media buying and representation, direct-mail and display advertising, standalone market-research firms, software vendors, captive in-house communications teams, shells, non-control financing vehicles, solo practitioners below the LMM band, and firms limited to isolated nonrepeat publicity projects.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market public relations agencies providing recurring or repeat outsourced communications campaigns, media relations, reputation and crisis counsel, public affairs, lobbying, political consulting, executive communications, stakeholder engagement, and related PR consulting to external clients.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix for four-digit NAICS 541800, not six-digit 541820, and O*NET describes representative PR occupations rather than every agency role. I map high exposure to brainstorming, research, media monitoring, first drafts, editing, releases, speeches, social posts, reporting, lists, and routine administration; I map lower exposure to relationship building, executive coaching, live media response, crisis judgment, lobbying, negotiation, and final reputational accountability. The broader industry contains more advertising, design, media-buying, and distribution work, so it may overstate visual-production exposure while understating 541820's concentration in PR specialists and senior counsel.
caveats: The occupation mix is for NAICS 541800 and includes advertising and related services with different workflows.
O*NET task importance does not measure time, wage weight, feasibility, or existing automation.
The Muck Rack survey reports individual tool use rather than the share of agency labor technically substitutable.
The injected labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and may be distorted where agency receipts include third-party campaign or event pass-through.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 541820 payroll occupation table combined with agency time-and-motion data by seniority, workflow, wage, and current AI use would materially change the wage-weighted estimate.
a.rationale: PR agencies perform large volumes of language, research, monitoring, and document work, and the reported use cases show AI already fits those workflows. Public relations specialists also spend substantial time maintaining external relationships, advising management, coaching spokespeople, and responding under time pressure, while managers own crisis plans and trusted relationships. Those human-intensive duties hold the current not-yet-automated exposed share near half at base.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for four-digit NAICS 541800 to grow 2.7% annually from 2024 to 2034, implying about 1.14 over five years, while PR-specialist employment grows 5% over the decade. I center below the broad industry output implication because 541800 includes advertising, media buying, and related services, while retaining modest growth due to continuing reputation, outreach, social-media, crisis, and stakeholder needs. IPRN's global member-agency optimism supports direction but likely overstates typical US LMM conditions.
caveats: The BLS output forecast is for NAICS 541800, not public relations agencies alone.
Occupation growth includes in-house PR staff and workers outside agencies.
IPRN is a global member network rather than a representative US LMM sample.
The estimate holds quality and the current service basket constant, excluding wholly new AI-visibility services.
d5.change_evidence: A six-digit BLS or Census real-output forecast for 541820, supported by inflation-adjusted US PR-agency billings, client retention, backlog, and budget surveys by end market, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Fast-moving public opinion, fragmented media, social platforms, crises, political and regulatory complexity, and reputation management sustain demand for the current service basket. PR budgets remain discretionary and cyclical, traditional media access is harder, and clients may reduce or postpone programs in downturns. AI self-service is reserved for the operator-required share rather than deducted again from demand quantity.

caveats: No source measures eligibility under the frozen recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM lens.
The injected firm count uses a 15.65% broad Business & Consumer Services margin and may misclassify high-margin counsel boutiques or lower-margin execution-heavy agencies into the EBITDA band.
The code includes lobbying and political consulting as well as conventional PR, creating customer and cyclicality variation.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit establishment census or commercial database segmented by independence, normalized EBITDA, contract duration, retainer share, repeat-client revenue, and principal service would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is intrinsically an external campaign and consulting service, and LMM-scale employer agencies commonly maintain ongoing client relationships through retainers and repeat programs. Ineligibility remains for project-only publicity shops, election-cycle entities without repeat commercial service, misclassified advertising or event businesses, captive units, and founder-personal practices whose work is not organizationally repeatable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541820 ($9,020,215,177) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541820 ($16,903,608,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $271,021 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541820); class <5: 6,209 firms, avg revenue/firm $468,889 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $73,381 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 927 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,829,861 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $286,373 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 551 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,315,470 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $518,871 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 348 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,294,121 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,611,030 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 73 firms, avg revenue/firm $38,745,397 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,063,655 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $82,824,510 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,962,036 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 421

caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share of PR service demand.
Agency survey statements about human judgment and strategic trust may reflect provider optimism.
Rapid improvement in autonomous monitoring, content generation, journalist targeting, sentiment analysis, and crisis simulation could push the value below the range.
Lobbying and political consulting are more operator-dependent than routine publicity, widening intra-code variation.
o.change_evidence: Client cohort data showing five-year agency-spend retention, insourcing, AI self-service, software substitution, and service unbundling by PR engagement type would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Clients can self-serve or buy software for basic releases, social copy, monitoring summaries, research, contact lists, and routine reporting. Most complex LMM-agency quantity should still require an accountable operator because media and government relationships, crisis response, political judgment, executive coaching, stakeholder negotiation, factual validation, brand risk, and trusted counsel depend on context and responsibility. The operator-required share is highest in crisis, public affairs, lobbying, regulated sectors, and reputation strategy and lowest in commodity content and distribution.

q.bridge: PRSA documents hourly fees against monthly or annual budgets, fixed project fees, and flat monthly retainers, but not the five-year retained share of AI benefit. I map fixed projects and flat retainers to higher initial retention, hourly budget work to lower retention because fewer hours reduce billings, and annual review or renewal to gradual customer sharing across all models. The guide is US and industry-specific but old and nonquantitative, so it may miss current performance pricing and stronger procurement pressure; the direction of error is uncertain.
caveats: The PRSA guide establishes fee structures but does not measure their prevalence or commercial retention.
The guide predates current AI adoption and modern procurement practices.
Retention varies sharply between hourly media-relations execution, fixed projects, crisis availability retainers, lobbying, and strategic counsel.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of current US 541820 contracts and account P&Ls showing fee model, utilization, realization, renewal pricing, scope expansion, client concessions, and margin before and after AI deployment would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Flat retainers and fixed projects allow agencies to retain near-term productivity through higher throughput or margin, especially when clients buy access, judgment, or outcomes rather than hours. Hourly-budget arrangements pass benefits through mechanically, and periodic retainer reviews, competitive pitches, AI-native entrants, and client transparency demands should share additional gains over five years. Differentiated crisis, public-affairs, and reputation counsel can retain more than commodity content work.

rho.bridge: Muck Rack surveyed 1,013 PR professionals across agency and in-house settings in late 2024, while IPRN surveyed a self-selected global network of independent agencies in 2026. I translate widespread individual use and regular member-agency deployment into the share of remaining exposed work operationalized within five years, discounting for incomplete policies, limited disclosure, privacy, plagiarism, output quality, client transparency demands, training, and workflow integration. IPRN members are likely more internationally connected and innovation-oriented than typical US LMM agencies, biasing adoption upward; smaller agencies may nevertheless implement faster than large governed organizations.
caveats: Tool use and self-reported speed or quality are not audited implementation or labor savings.
Muck Rack mixes agency and in-house professionals and does not isolate LMM firms.
IPRN covers member agencies across more than 22 countries and does not state a respondent count on the fetched page.
Current high adoption reduces the remaining not-yet-implemented opportunity even as it supports future workflow acceptance.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal US PR-agency survey reporting production hours, utilization, rework, client approvals, policy coverage, and headcount avoided by workflow before and after AI deployment would materially move this value.
rho.rationale: Three-quarters of surveyed PR professionals already used GenAI in 2025, with common use in brainstorming, drafting, editing, and research, and IPRN reported regular use across most member agencies in 2026. The behavioral barrier is therefore low. Implementation will deepen, but confidential client information, factual error, brand voice, disclosure, legal and political sensitivity, and the need for senior review prevent full conversion of exposure into dependable labor avoidance.

s5.bridge: Gallup found that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while PRovoke Media identified 99 PR-firm transactions in 2024, including many small targets. I use the owner-plan rate as a broad anchor and the observed sector activity as evidence that agency transfers clear, then discount gifts, internal transfers, minority investments, non-US transactions, sub-LMM micro deals, unexecuted intentions, and founder-dependent firms that cannot complete a qualifying control sale. The deal count has no eligible-firm denominator and may bias upward because it is global and includes firms with only a minor PR component.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than PR agencies or the LMM band.
PRovoke's transaction universe is not restricted to the United States, control deals, or the frozen EBITDA band.
Many observed targets below $6 million in fees are likely below the LMM EBITDA threshold.
Founder and rainmaker dependence can make nominal agencies difficult to transfer without long earn-outs.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, PRovoke, or Davis+Gilbert data isolating completed US 541820 control transactions and failed sale processes by eligible-firm denominator over five years would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: PR agency M&A is demonstrably active, and the concentration of 2024 deals among smaller firms shows a functioning buyer market below the large holding companies. Employer agencies can transfer client rosters, sector expertise, teams, and methods, but founder relationships, change-of-control churn, earn-outs, political affiliations, and key-person reputational capital reduce the qualifying five-year probability.

principal_driver: Large volumes of writing, research, monitoring, editing, and reporting create implementable AI leverage inside recurring agency relationships.
principal_weakness: Commodity PR production is easy for clients and AI-native competitors to self-serve, constraining both operator-required demand and retained pricing benefit.

caveats: The labor-opportunity primitives are directionally grounded but not quantitatively identified: the four-digit occupation mix and O*NET tasks do not establish a wage-weighted six-digit exposure share, and adoption surveys do not measure dependable labor avoidance.

The rho bridge uses current tool adoption as a proxy for five-year operationalization of remaining exposed work; the researcher discloses the gap, but audited implementation, rework, utilization, and headcount evidence is absent.

The q base is an assumption-heavy proxy. The PRSA guide verifies fee structures and renewal mechanics but provides no current fee-model prevalence or empirical pass-through, so the base case's capped C factor should not be read as substantiated retention.

The availability factor rests on an honest estimate of eligible firms and a broad succession proxy: Gallup includes gifts and transfers across all employer businesses, while PRovoke's global transaction universe includes sub-LMM, non-control, and partly PR-adjacent deals without an eligible denominator.

Demand evidence is broader than the lens. The BLS output series is four-digit 541800, the occupation outlook includes in-house roles, and the IPRN survey is a self-selected global member network rather than representative US LMM evidence.

The code definition spans conventional PR, lobbying, political consulting, and PR consulting. Marking the lens non-heterogeneous understates meaningful differences in cyclicality, human relationship intensity, fee models, and AI self-service risk, although the packet acknowledges this variation and provides wide ranges.

The central e and o values remain explicitly labeled estimates with no direct population measurement, and the margin-bridged firm count is also an estimate; the resulting MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action are therefore appropriate.

sources_audited.note: The Census manual supports the 541820 definition and expressly includes lobbying, political consulting, and PR consulting, but it does not measure the scored eligibility or operator-required shares.

The May 2023 BLS table supports the stated occupation and wage mix for NAICS 541800, including PR managers and specialists, writers and editors, market-research analysts, project managers, and office support; it does not isolate six-digit 541820 or measure AI exposure.

O*NET supports the cited specialist tasks, including media response, releases, social content, relationship maintenance, management advice, coaching, and strategy, but task importance is not time, wage weight, substitutability, or operator-required demand.

O*NET supports the cited manager duties, including crisis plans, executive speeches and interviews, client-government-media relationships, budgets, supervision, and public response, but it does not quantify automation exposure or future operator-required demand.

Axios reports Muck Rack's late-2024 survey of 1,013 PR professionals, 75% GenAI use, and the cited use cases and policy/training concerns. The mixed agency/in-house survey measures use and perceptions, not labor avoidance or five-year implementation.

The IPRN page reports 82% regular AI use, 67% forecasting growth, conditional client acceptance and transparency, and human judgment and strategic trust as differentiators. It is a global member-agency survey across more than 22 countries and does not disclose a respondent count on the page.

The PRSA guide supports ongoing and project relationships, annual renewal, hourly fees against monthly or yearly budgets, project fees, and flat monthly retainers. It is old and supplies neither fee-model prevalence nor a measured retained share of AI benefits.

PRovoke supports 99 transactions in 2024, 62% involving firms below $6 million in revenue or fees, and the prominence of independent and private-equity buyers. Its global, partly PR-adjacent transaction count has no eligible-firm denominator and is not limited to qualifying US control sales.

Gallup supports that 22% of US employer-business owners planned within five years to sell or transfer ownership. The measure spans all employer businesses and includes transfers rather than completed qualifying sales.
BLS supports the cited 2024-2034 real-output projection for NAICS 541800 from $197.3 billion to $257.3 billion in chained 2017 dollars, or 2.7% annually, but not six-digit 541820 demand.

BLS supports 5% projected PR-specialist employment growth from 2024 to 2034 and the cited reputation, online-news, community/customer-relations, and social-media drivers. The occupation includes in-house roles and workers outside PR agencies.
